original_abstract:
The selection of appropriate stimuli to elicit specific emotional states is a cornerstone of affective research. While several standardized databases for auditory, linguistic, and visual materials currently exist and have been utilized extensively in behavioral and neuroimaging studies, they are not without limitations. Recent critiques have highlighted issues such as insufficient stimuli in certain semantic categories and the outdated, low-quality resolution of many available images. To address these shortcomings, we developed the Nencki Affective Picture System (NAPS), a comprehensive collection of 1,356 high-quality, realistic photographs categorized into five distinct groups: people, faces, animals, objects, and landscapes. 

Normative affective ratings were gathered from 204 participants (primarily of European descent) using computerized semantic slider scales to assess the dimensions of valence, arousal, and approach-avoidance. We validated these ratings through comparisons with the International Affective Picture System (IAPS) and the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM). Furthermore, we provide detailed objective physical parameters for each image, including luminance, contrast, and entropy. By offering a diverse range of high-resolution stimuli alongside standardized ratings and image properties, NAPS provides a robust tool that allows researchers to precisely tailor their stimulus selection to their specific experimental goals. The database is freely available to the scientific community for noncommercial research upon request at http://naps.nencki.gov.pl.
humanized_abstract:
Choosing appropriate stimuli to induce different emotional states is one of the basic tasks in affective research. There are a few well-known databases of auditory, language and visual stimuli that are widely used in behavioral and neuroimaging studies. However, there are a few drawbacks to the existing databases, such as a lack of stimuli in some semantic categories and a low quality and old resolution of the images. In the present study, we introduce the Nencki Affective Picture System (NAPS) - a set of 1356 high quality, realistic photographs designed to elicit different emotional states. The images are divided into five basic semantic categories: people, faces, animals, objects and landscapes.

Normative affective ratings were collected from 204 (predominantly white Caucasian) participants, using computerized semantic slider scales assessing valence, arousal and approach-avoidance. These ratings are compared to IAPS and SAM ratings, and the objective physical properties of each image (luminance, contrast, entropy) are also provided. The goal of this resource is to provide a large and variable set of high quality images along with the corresponding normative affective ratings and physical properties of the images, and to enable researchers to select precisely the materials they require for any particular study. The database can be obtained for non-commercial use upon request to naps@nencki.gov.pl.
